title: How Enterprise Executives Play a Pivotal Role in the Year 2000 Solution
author: Aberdeen Group
source_file: 1996 How Enterprise Executives Play a Pivotal Role in the Year 2000 Solution VP.pdf
abstract: Aberdeen Group argues that the Year 2000 (Y2K) problem is fundamentally a competitive strategy issue, not merely a technical one, and that senior executives — not just IT departments — must take leadership roles. The study contends that non-Y2K-compliant enterprises will suffer competitive disadvantage as customers, partners (e.g., Wal-Mart), and regulators require proof of Y2K readiness, and prescribes a proactive executive response with trusted external assistance.
importance_rationale: Among the earliest calls to engage senior executives in Y2K remediation as a strategic imperative rather than a purely technical problem; Aberdeen's framing of Y2K as a competitive advantage issue was ahead of mainstream awareness in October 1996.
relevance_rationale: The framework for executive leadership in systemic technology risk management remains directly applicable to modern analogues (cybersecurity, AI governance, supply chain digitization); Y2K-specific content is historical.
prescience_rationale: Aberdeen's prediction that enterprises would face crash-buying sprees in 1997–1999 proved correct; Y2K spending reached $300-600 billion globally. The prediction that non-compliant firms would face competitive disadvantage was broadly validated. The 'disastrous implications' prediction was partially wrong — the transition passed with fewer failures than feared due to the remediation effort Aberdeen prescribed.
